coredata.dc:title: Colorimetric measurement of Fe3+ using a functional paper-based sensor based on catalytic oxidation of gold nanoparticles
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Jun-jie
Wang, Xian-feng
Huo, Dan-qun
Hou, Chang-jun
Fa, Huan-bao
Yang, Mei
Zhang, Liang
coredata.dc:description: Direct detection of Fe3+ ions is of significant importance in view of both clinical and environmental applications. In the present study, we presented the construction of a paper-based colorimetric sensor by alternative modification of commercial cellulose paper with TiO2 membrane as intermediate layer, hydrophobic/hydrophilic coating to obtain uniform diffusion, and decoration of –NH2 and –SH groups for gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) immobilization. After optimization of fabrication and detection conditions, the colorimetric sensor showed a linear concentration-dependent colorimetric response (ΔG) in the presence of Fe3+ concentrations ranging from 1.0 to 37μM (R2 >0.98). The detection limit was calculated to be 0.85μM (3δ) with good selectivity. Besides, the sensor also showed high resistance to AuNPs leaking in aqueous solutions, and demonstrated consistent measurement of Fe3+ in serum samples. Those results suggested that as-prepared sensor strip is potential useful for Fe3+ ion determination in clinical and environmental samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Iron ions
Gold nanoparticles
Colorimetric strips
Serum

Fabrication of the functionalized cellulose filter paper
Analytical performance of the paper-based sensor
Application of the sensor to detect Fe3+ in spiked serum samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of the procedure to fabricate the Fe3+-specific colorimetric sensor on commercial cellulose filter paper.

TEMimage (A) and UV–vis absorption (D) of the chitosan stabilized gold nano-particles, SEM surface images of cellulose fiber before (B) and after TiO2 modification (C), X-ray diffraction pattern of TiO2 coated cellulose paper (E) and hydrophobic measurement of that after HTEDS decoration (F).
Influence of the concentration of MPTMOS (A), APTEOS (B), and dipping times of AuNPs solution (C).
Influence of the concentration of HCl (A) and thiourea (B) on the colorimetric response of the sensor.
Influence of the temperature (A) and reaction time (B).
Response of the colorimetric sensor upon addition of Fe3+ of different concentrations and corresponding real images.
Real images of the sensor after interaction with common heavy metal ions (A) and corresponding colorimetric response (B).
Detection of Fe3+ concentration in serum samples.

head.ce:title: Colorimetric measurement of Fe3+ using a functional paper-based sensor based on catalytic oxidation of gold nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Biorheology Science and Technology, Ministry of Education, College of Bioengineering, Chongqing University, Chongqing, 400044, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Biorheology Science and Technology,

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Key Laboratory of Fundamental Science of Micro/Nano-Device and System Technology

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Engineering Research Center of Solid-State Brewing

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Key Laboratory of Biorheology Science and Technology, Ministry of Education, College of Bioengineering, Chongqing University, Chongqing, 400044, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Those authors contribute equally to the article.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Direct detection of Fe3+ ions is of significant importance in view of both clinical and environmental applications. In the present study, we presented the construction of a paper-based colorimetric sensor by alternative modification of commercial cellulose paper with TiO2 membrane as intermediate layer, hydrophobic/hydrophilic coating to obtain uniform diffusion, and decoration of –NH2 and –SH groups for gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) immobilization. After optimization of fabrication and detection conditions, the colorimetric sensor showed a linear concentration-dependent colorimetric response (ΔG) in the presence of Fe3+ concentrations ranging from 1.0 to 37μM (R2 >0.98). The detection limit was calculated to be 0.85μM (3δ) with good selectivity. Besides, the sensor also showed high resistance to AuNPs leaking in aqueous solutions, and demonstrated consistent measurement of Fe3+ in serum samples. Those results suggested that as-prepared sensor strip is potential useful for Fe3+ ion determination in clinical and environmental samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Fe3+ is among the most important metal ions that closely related to a variety of physiological and pathological events [1,2]. Under optimal concentrations, it plays indispensable roles in biological processes like DNA synthesis, respiration, oxygen transport, and also formation of some neurotransmitters and hormones, and so on [3]. However, when excess amount of free iron presents in cell, it would lead to oxidization and damage of the protein, nucleic acid and lipids [2]. Consequently, overdosed Fe3+ would induce serious health problems or diseases including to anemia, dysfunction of organs and even cancers [4–6]. Therefore, facile and reliable measurement of Fe3+ concentrations of extraordinary import in not only clinical but also environmental applications [7].

Accordingly, effective measurement of Fe3+ amount in related samples have been presented using a number of analytical methods, which include Inductively Coupled Plasma-Mass Spectrometry (ICP-MS), [8,9] atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) [10,11], anodic stripping voltammetry [12], and fluorescent spectroscopy [13,14], and etc. Although those techniques demonstrated good accuracy and sensitivity, their application was hindered due to unavoidable limitations including high cost, requirement of professional operation, sophisticated pretreatment, and also no accessibility to real-time field monitoring. In view of above-mentioned problems, colorimetric sensors offered an effective alternative to deal with the dilemma for the measurement of metal ions [15–17]. Generally, they are ease of construction, convenient data acquisition even by naked eyes, and also easy to be integrated to form sensor array that is accessible for multi-components detection [18–20]. In particular, colorimetric sensors based on the gold particles are becoming more and more attractive due merits of AuNPs, which including high surface area, controllable morphology, extraordinary localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR), biological compatibility, and also easy functionalization [21–23]. Although the majority of AuNPs based colorimetric sensor depends on target induced aggregation of AuNPs in solution, interfering counterparts like protein and nucleotide also lead to more or less aggregation, thus result in false-positive measurement in complex biological samples. In a previous report, Chen and coworkers presented the first non-aggregation based colorimetric probe for label-free detection of Pb2+ based on the catalytic AuNPs leaching strategy [24]. This analytical method has also been realized using nitrocellulose membrane (NCM) [25] and electrospun polyamide-6/nitrocellulose (PA-6/NC) nano-fibers/nets (NFN) membrane [26]. However, it is recognized that immobilized AuNPs at the membrane surface are vulnerable to leakage upon washing due to unstable binding of AuNPs in the interface. Therefore, false-positive result would also be exclude.

In the present study, we present a more stable paper-based sensor fabricated by successive coating of a TiO2 intermediate layer, hydrophobic/hydrophilic modification, and decoration of functional –NH2 and –SH groups to allow for strong chitosan-stabilized AuNPs binding. In such a design, sample detection can be realized by droplet addition at the sensor surface rather than soaking in solution. We thus envision as-prepared sensor would provide more stable and reliable determination of Fe3+ in solutions.

Silylating agents including hexatriemethoxysilane (HTEDS), 3-mercaptopropyl trimethoxysilane (MPTMOS), and 3-Aminopropyl triethoxysilane (APTEOS),tetrachloroauric (III) acid (HAuCl4), chitosan, thiourea, and other metal-salts were obtained from the Aladdin reagent company (Shanghai, China).Tetrabutyltitanate (TBT), conentrated HCl (HCl), ethanol, acetic acid (HAc), NaOH and all other chemicals of analytical grade were purchased from Chuangdong Chemical Company (Chongqing, China). All chemicals were used without further purification and solutions are prepared with deionized water (Millipore) unless mentioned otherwise. Commercial cellulose filter paper (Φ=7.0cm) was purchased from Shuangquan Filter Paper Ltd. Co. (Hangzhou, China).

A PHS-3C pH detector (Shanghai Jingke Equipment, Shanghai, China), ultrasonic atomizer apparatus (Shanghai Yuyue Medical Equipment Company, Shanghai, China) were used throughout the study. The SEM and TEM characterization were performed with a FEI Nova400 system and a FEI Tecnai G2 TF20 UT instrument with a field emission gun, respectively. The UV/vis spectrum were recorded using a Lamda-900 spectrophotometer. Photographic results were recorded using an Epson Perfection V10 scanner and analyzed with the ImageJ software (NIH).

Chitosan-stabilized gold nano-particles was synthesized according to former studies with slight modification [27]. Briefly, chitosan solution of 3mg/mL was prepared with 1% acetic acid by stirring overnight. After addition of 2mL HAuCl4 solution (25mM) into 50mL as-prepared chitosan solution, the mixture was incubated at 70°C for 10min, and then heated to 100°C and kept stirring until the color turned into deep red from brownish. The AuNP solution was stored at 4°C under darkness. BSA coating of AuNP was realized by direct mixing the chitosan-stabilized AuNPs with BSA stock solution to a concentration of 3μM using magnetically stirring for 30min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the cellulose filter paper-based sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Typically, to 50mL absolute ethanol 10 pieces of commercial cellulose paper (CP) were immersed for no less than 2h before dried in an oven at 50°C. They were then soaking into 50mL mixed solution containing ethanol, TBT, and acetic acid (V/V/V, 10:3:1) in a culture dish. It was allowed to hydrolyze for 30min under shaking (100npr) at ambient conditions, and then removed the solutions remaining in the paper surface by tissue papers, after which kept at 60°C for 1h before solutions evaporated completely. Those steps were repeated for three times before the filter papers were subsequently hydrolyzed in 200mL ultrapure water for 4h at 140°C, and finally dried for 2h to obtain TiO2 modified filter papers (TCPs). The TCPs were then immersed into 10mL aqueous HTDES solution (5%) for 10min, dry in oven (70°C). After successive addition of 5mL 20% acetic acid solution, they were kept in 70°C to allow for final hydrophobic modification for 6h.

The hydrophobic papers were then placed in a self-made masking model for ultra-violet exposure for 2h, so that pro-determined positions (cycles with 5cm diameter) of the paper changed into hydrophilic. 3μL APTEOS (3%/V%) ethanol solution was dipped at center of the hydrophilic positions and then 20mL deionized water to initiate the silylating reaction in an oven at 100°C for 30min. The papers were washed with deionized water to remove unreacted APTEOS, and then dried in oven. After that 3μL MPTMOS (V%, 3%) ethanol solution was applied to repeat the procedures similar to that of ASTEOS. NH2- and SH- modified TCPs were kept in N2 saturated containers before subsequent modification. To fabricate the paper-based sensor, 0.8μL AuNPs-BSA solution was slowly added onto the modified TCP. After absorption of the solution, deionized water was applied to wash away free AuNPs-BSA, and dried with an electrical drier. This procedure can be repeated for given times and they were cut into small squares (0.7cm*0.7cm).

Similar method was applied to decorate the paper with –HN2 and –SH groups using MPTMOS and APTEOS solutions with a volume percentage of 1%,2%,3%,4% and 5%, respectively. A small piece of the sensor was immersed into a solution containing 0.15M thiourea, 0.15M HCl and 50μM for 15min at 50°C. The paper sensors were scanned to obtain real images before and after reaction. R, G and B information of real images were obtained using ImageJ and, ΔG value, which is calculated by direct deduction of the average G after reaction to that before, was used to quantify the colorimetric response. Other detection conditions were optimized by varying the concentration of thiourea (25, 50, 75, 100, 125, 150, 175, 200, 225, and 250mM), HCl (10, 25, 50, 75, 100, 150, 200, 225, 250, 300, and 350mM), temperature (20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, and 90°C) and reaction time, and etc.

Stability and interference of related components were investigated in the presence of interfering compounds using optimized condition. Typically, the concentrations of all the metal ions were set at 50μM. Fe3+ spiked samples was prepared by dilution of the serum samples with Fe3+ stock solution to pre-determined concentrations (0, 5, 10, and 20μM). Measurements of the response were similar to that for standard Fe3+ solutions expect that the sensor was washed thoroughly with deionized water and dried in oven before final scanning. The accurate Fe3+ concentrations were detected using inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP–MS), and subsequently used to calculate recovery value.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the functionalized cellulose filter paper
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 1 illustrated the construction procedure of the paper-based sensor using functional cellulose filter paper. We first modified commercial filter paper with TiO2 film according to our former study [28]. As in shown in Fig. 1 B, cellulose fibers in micro size furcated into bundled nanofibers after immersion in ethanol, and gave the filter paper with a rough surface morphology preferred for chemical coating of titanium film. Studies suggested that the titanium membrane at the fiber surface provided an intermediate layer suitable for subsequent decoration using silylating reagents due to with good chemical activity [29,30]. After TiO2 coating and calcination at 140°C, a rough surface composed with nano TiO2 bumps was observed (Fig. 1C). It would further increase the surface-to-volume ratio and activate the inert cellulose surface for following-up modification. Raman characterization shows that there are strong bands at around 143, 196, 390, 516, and 639cm−1 after modification of TiO2 and successive calcination at 140°C (Fig. S2). These peaks match well with the characteristic peak of anatase TiO2 [31,32], and seemly agrees with the finding that the TiO2 coating on filter paper might be crystallized merely by hydrolyzing in water at 140°C for 4h [33]. However, in the XRD patterns of TCP (Fig. 1E), we observed no clear featured anatase (1 0 1) facet at 2θ=25.3°or the rutile (1 1 0) facet at 2θ=27.5°. Comparing the diffraction peaks of the XRD patterns with the standard XRD pattern database, it was found that the 2θ=22.9° matched well with that of the (1 0 1) facet in Ti6O11 phase (PDF 50-0788). Besides, we have also found the peaks of 34.5°, 47.6° and 55.7° and they are consistent with the (2 0 10), (0 0 10) and (3 0 9) facets [34]. Therefore, it is more likely that surface TiO2 coating in present study should be indexed as the Magneli phase structure of Ti6O11 rather anatase phase of TiO2 [35].

In previous reports, heavy metal ions measurement using paper-based strips were all obtained by direct immersion of the paper into samples solutions in the presence of other catalyst. Therefore, it is easy to result in false-positive observation due to AuNPs leaching. Herein, we endeavored to realize direct reaction of sample droplet with the functional surface of paper sensor rather than the socking methods. Due to surface hydrophilicity of cellulose paper and that after TiO2 modification, aqueous samples in the interface would diffuse rapidly before the AuNPs oxidation completes. So it is essential to change the surface properties of paper substrates to allow for modulation of dispersion and prolonged contacting time of aqueous samples in the interface. After TiO2 coating, the paper was further decorated with HTDES to make the surface hydrophobic, and then treated with ultraviolet light to obtain partial hydrophilicity in certain area with given size. With such design, aqueous sample of given volume would be restricted to form droplet and disperse within given area with uniform size (see Fig. S3). It ensures homogenous change of final color in consistent intensity thus impart the colorimetric sensor a better reproducibility.

The Raman spectra of HTEDS grafted paper (Fig. S2), whether treated by ultraviolet light or not, showed clear peaks around 420 and 450cm−1, probably resulted from the presence of Si-O-Si structure resulted from TPED grafting [36]. After ultraviolet treatment new peak at around 1700cm−1 was observed, which is indicative of the presence of υ(CO). This might be attributed to ultraviolet induced cleavage of the CC bond and generation of CO [37]. Aqueous samples diffuse and dry quickly at the surface of filter paper, but after grafting with TPED, the long hydrophobic alkyl chains restrict the diffusion and the contact angle was measured to be about 127.4° (Fig. S3). Decoration of −SH and NH2 functional groups provided not only anchoring sites for AuNPs binding but also modulation of the surface hydrophilicity/hydrophobicity. As is shown in Fig. 1A, TEM image of as-prepared AuNPs stabilized by chitosan were in globose appearance with multi-dispersed sizes (average 14nm), and corresponding ultraviolet-visible absorption indicated the maximum absorption at around 520nm. Zeta potential measurement suggested that the chitosan capped AuNPs carried a negatively charge of about −10mV. After incubation with BSA, it turned into positively charged at around +39mV. As mentioned above, the chitosan solution for synthesizing AuNPs was prepared with 1% acetic acid and the origin pH value is about 2.8. The isoelectric point of BSA is 4.6, and therefore BSA has a positive net charge at lower pH environment. So the significant change of zeta potential could be attributed to the binding of positively charged BSA to negatively charged surfaces of AuNPs. Besides, the large molecular structure of BSA would further improve the stability of immobilized AuNPs with minimal aggregation possibility.

The influence of APTEOS and MPTMOS concentration on the colorimetric response to Fe3+ was first investigated. Typically, the hydrophilic area in TCP was first treated with a small amount of APTEOS and then initiated its hydrolysis to decorate the surface with appropriate –NH2. As is shown in Fig. 2 A, increasing concentration of MPTMOS from 1% to 5% (v%) led to the colorimetric response (ΔG) first increase and then decrease. We found that the –NH2 groups would give rise to the hydrophilicity while –SH increases the hydrophobicity thus impact the immobilization of AuNPs. The largest response was observed at a concentration of 3% for MPTMOS. Similar change of colorimetric response was observed by varying the APTEOS concentration. When APTEOS concentrations increase from 1% to 3%, the response was growing but that over 3% led to a gradual decline of ΔG (Fig. 2B). It might due to that excessed –NH2 groups occupy the sites for –SH and lessen the binding of AuNPs. So we investigated the dipping/washing times of AuNPs solution to further improve the sensor performance. It suggested that the ΔG value increased monotonously as the dipping cycles increased from 1 to 5 (Fig. 2C). After that, no further change in ΔG was observed. 5 times repeated dipping/washing of AuNPs solution was thus considered favorable for final sensor fabrication.

Accordingly, Fe3+ can speed up the catalytic leaching of gold nanoparticles in the presence of thiourea [38] or hydrogen peroxide [39]. In the present study, we use thiourea to obtain AuNPs oxidation by Fe3+ according to the following mechanism [40,41].
A u ( 0 ) + 2 S C
(
N H
2
)
2
→ A u
[ S C
(
N H
2
)
2
]
2 +
+
e −

This reaction would proceed very slowly unless in the presence of HCl. Therefore, impact of thiourea and HCl was investigated to optimize the detection conditions. As is shown in Fig. 3 A, the concentration of HCl is crucial to initiate the catalytic reaction. In the absence of HCl in the sample solution, the colorimetric response can be hardly observed in about 1h even after addition of over amount Fe3+ solutions. When a growing concentration of HCl applied from 0.01M to 0.35M, resultant colorimetric response increased to reach a maximum value at 0.2M. Overdosed HCl would lead to no further increase of sensor response thus 0.2M HCl in sample was determined favorable. The influcence of thiourea is somehow different from that of HCl. It was found that increasing thiourea concentration from 0.025 to 0.15M led to gradual increase of colorimetric response. But further increase from 0.15 to 0.25M resulted a clear decline. In view of those observation, the final thiourea concentration was set as 0.15M in following-up experiments.

Former studies demonstrated that temperature and reaction time would also exert significant influence on the catalytic oxidation of AuNPs because they either affect the dynamic kinetics or determine the final equilibrium of the reaction. Results shown in Fig. 4 A plotted the temperature-dependent sensor response from 20 to 90°C. The leaching reaction rate is positively proportional to the temperature increase from 20 to 60°C, after which no clear increase in ΔG was observed. It indicated the balance of increasing collision frequency at 60°C, and this temperature was thus selected to optimize the impact of reaction time. The liquid at the sensor surface was absorb by tissue paper at predetermined time, and then dried to measure the response. As is shown in Fig. 4B, there was a strongly correlation between the sensor response. When the reaction time growing from 2min to 20min, an equilibrium of leaching reaction was obtained at 12min. After that, no significant improved response can be observed even prolonging the reaction to naturally evaporation of the sample droplet. So that 12min is long enough to complete the leaching reaction and thus regarded as preferred reaction time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the paper-based sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5 shows the Fe3+-dependent AuNPs leaching using increasing Fe3+ samples from 0.1 to 37μM. In the presence of increasing amount of Fe3+ ions, the intensity of the original reddish color diminishes gradually. It was found that change of the color intensity can be distinguished even by naked eyes when the Fe3+ concentration higher than 2.5μM. The AuNPs immobilized at the paper surface would be completely oxidized if overdosed Fe3+ ions higher than 50μM, which is the upper limit for the detection. A linear correlation between resultant ΔG value and Fe3+ concentration was observed in a range from 1.0 to 37μM (R2 >0.98). And the theoretical detection limit was calculated to be around 0.85μM (3δ).

In order to evaluate the selectivity of the present sensor, investigation of sensor response in the presence of a number of possible interfering compounds including K+, Na+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Ca2+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Ge4+, Co2+, Ce3+, Ni2+, Ag2+, and Sn2+ was conducted. As is summarized in Fig. 6 , none of the tested metal ions result in visible color fading of the AuNPs immobilized paper. And the resultant alteration of ΔG value were in a limited range significantly lower than that of Fe3+ ions. Therefore, it is reasonable to claim the good selectivity of the present sensor.
In addition, stability is also a crucial factor that determines the performance of sensors.

Since AuNPs leaking is extensively observed on AuNPs immobilized functional membranes/films due unstable interaction with interfacial functional groups, we investigate the stability of the present paper sensor from different batches by direct immersion into Fe3+ free solutions for different time. As shown in Fig. S3, no significant variation was observed in a reaction time even 7-times longer than desired, demonstrating the resistance of the functional paper to leakage of gold nano-particles. Besides, although fluctuation of response for different sensors in different batches was observed, the average signal remains consistent after reaction of Fe3+ in the same concentration. Since the standard deviation is no more than 5%, the present sensor is stable enough for real applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the sensor to detect Fe3+ in spiked serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: Since Fe3+ is frequently observed in serum, we lastly evaluated the feasibility of as-prepared sensor to measurement Fe3+ ion in serum samples. Optimized amount of serum samples was spiked with different amount of Fe3+ and analyzed according to procedure mentioned before. As is summarized in Table 1 , the sensor demonstrated acceptable recovery between 95% and 98% in the presence of with spiked Fe3+ from 5.0 to 20.0μM. Typically, the average Fe3+ concentration in serum samples was calculated to be 27.14μM, consistent with those detected by other techniques [42,43], indicating good feasibility for real sample measurement using as-prepared paper-based sensor.

In summary, we presented the construction of a paper-based colorimetric sensor by alternative modification of commercial cellulose paper with TiO2 membrane, hydrophobic/hydrophilic coating and decoration of –SH and –NH2 groups. Those strategies not only realized droplet detection of aqueous Fe3+ samples but also reduced leakage of immobilized AuNPs at interface upon direct soaking in solution. As-prepared colorimetric sensor demonstrated feasibility for direct detection of Fe3+ concentration in serum samples with good consistence. Thus offered a potentially efficient tool for the measurement of this ions in clinical and environmental samples. More importantly, the construction strategy can be further utilized for the fabrication of membrane-based sensor to determine other metal ions in the solid/liquid interface.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge the financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31171684), Chongqing Graduate Student Research Innovation Project (CYB14013), Key Technologies R&D Program of China (No. 2014BAD07B02), the workstation in Sichuan Province GY2015-01, and sharing fund of Chongqing university’s large equipment.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2016.09.039.

ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

Gold nanorods as colorful chromogenic substrates for semiquantitative detection of nucleic acids, proteins and small molecules with the naked eye
Anal. Chem.
Reaction chemistry of gold leaching in thiourea solution for a Turkish gold ore
Hydrometallurgy
A review of gold leaching in acid thiourea solutions
Miner. Process. Extr. Metall. Rev.
Enhanced fluorescence of chitosan based on size change of micelles and application to directly selective detecting Fe 3+ in humanserum
Biosens. Bioelectron.
Determination of cadmium, cobalt, copper, iron, manganese, and zinc in thyroid glands of patients with diagnosed nodular goitre using ion chromatography
J. Chromatogr. B

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Li Jun-jie is presently a Ph. D. student in College of Bioengineering at Chongqing University, People’s Republic of China. His research interests include the development of new nanomaterials for application in optoelectronic devices and analysis.
Wang Xian-feng is currently studying for master degree in Bioengineering College of Chongqiong University. His research interests lie in sensors and technology of biochemical sensors detections.

Huo Dan-qun is a professor in College of Bioengineering, Chongqing University, China. Her current research interests include the development of chemical sensors and sensor-array, Electrochemistry and analytical microsystems.

Hou Chang-jun is presently a full professor in College of Bioengineering at Chongqing University, People's Republic of China. He received his Ph. D. degree from Chongqing University, China, in 2004. His scientific interests are the design and construction of biochemical sensing systems based on porphyrins and other sensitive materials.

Fa Huan-bao is presently an associated professor in College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Chongqing University. He received his Ph. D. degree from Jilin University, China, in 2006. His research interest focuses on the development of novel nanomaterials and their applications in Electrochemistry.

Yang Mei is presently an associated professor in College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Chongqing University. She received her Ph. D. degree from Sichuan University, China, in 2009. Her research interest focuses on the development of novel nanomaterials for application.
Zhang Liang is a senior engineer of Luzhou Laojiao Group CO. Ltd. His research interest focuses on the food safety and quality control.
